enrichment.short_description: A completely free website analytics tool from Microsoft that shows how visitors behave on a site through heatmaps, session recordings, and AI-powered insights.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Clarity Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Clarity review covering heatmaps, session recordings, AI Copilot insights, setup, and how this free analytics tool compares to paid alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Clarity is a free, unlimited user-behavior analytics platform launched by Microsoft on October 28, 2020, after an internal project that began around 2018 to study visitor behavior on properties like Bing. It gives website owners heatmaps, session recordings, and aggregate behavioral metrics without any paid tier, seat limit, traffic cap, or feature paywall. Clarity works by dropping a lightweight JavaScript tracking snippet on a site (installed manually, through Google Tag Manager, or via one-click integrations with WordPress, Shopify, Shopify Plus, Wix, and Squarespace) that records anonymized user sessions and aggregates click, scroll, and movement data into visual heatmaps. It also automatically flags friction signals such as rage clicks (repeated rapid clicking that signals user frustration), dead clicks (clicks on non-interactive elements), excessive scrolling, and quick backs. A built-in AI feature called Clarity Copilot lets teams ask natural-language questions about their data, such as identifying the top friction point on a given page, and get a generated answer instead of manually digging through dashboards. Clarity also integrates with Google Analytics and Google Analytics 360, letting teams cross-reference Clarity session recordings against GA traffic segments. The product is entirely free because Microsoft uses the anonymized, aggregated behavioral data to improve its own machine learning models and understand broader digital usage trends, while stating that individual user data is not sold to third parties. There are no Pro, Business, or Enterprise tiers, no user limits, and no website limits, which differentiates Clarity sharply from paid session-recording competitors such as Hotjar, FullStory, Crazy Egg, and Mouseflow, all of which cap free plans by session volume and gate advanced features like unlimited recording history or filtering behind paid subscriptions. Clarity's target audience includes small businesses, freelancers, marketing teams, UX researchers, and SEO practitioners who want qualitative behavior data to complement quantitative tools like Google Analytics or Google Search Console, without adding another line item to a software budget. Because it has no revenue model tied to feature gating, Clarity is commonly recommended as a low-risk first step into behavioral analytics before a team decides whether a paid, more feature-rich competitor is worth the cost. Its main limitations relative to paid tools are a simpler interface with fewer built-in segmentation and reporting options, and data retention policies that are shorter than some enterprise-grade paid platforms. Clarity is widely cited as one of the most adopted free analytics add-ons for WordPress and Shopify stores, and Microsoft has continued to expand it with features like custom tags, filters, and deeper e-commerce platform integrations since its 2020 launch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Clarity is a free website analytics tool that captures how real visitors interact with a page. It began as an internal Microsoft project studying user behavior on properties like Bing before its public release on October 28, 2020, and it has remained free with no paid tier since launch.

Unlike traditional analytics tools that report pageviews and conversion funnels, Clarity focuses on qualitative behavior: heatmaps that show where people click and scroll, and session recordings that replay individual visits. It is designed to sit alongside Google Analytics rather than replace it, filling in the 'why' behind the numbers a traffic dashboard already shows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Clarity's core features are heatmaps (click, scroll, and area heatmaps aggregated across sessions), session recordings (anonymized replays of individual visitor sessions), and automatic frustration signals like rage clicks, dead clicks, and excessive scrolling that flag likely UX problems without manual review.

Clarity Copilot, an AI assistant built into the dashboard, lets users ask plain-language questions about site behavior and receive a generated summary instead of manually filtering recordings. Clarity also supports custom tags and segments for filtering sessions by URL, device, browser, or custom JavaScript-triggered events, and it connects to Google Analytics and Google Analytics 360 so recordings can be cross-referenced against existing GA segments. One-click installation is available for WordPress, Shopify, Shopify Plus, Wix, Squarespace, and WooCommerce, in addition to manual script or Google Tag Manager installation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Microsoft Clarity has a single pricing tier: free. There are no Pro, Business, or Enterprise plans, no caps on the number of sites, team members, or monthly sessions tracked, and every feature, including heatmaps, session recordings, Clarity Copilot, and custom tags, is available to every account at no cost.

Microsoft sustains Clarity as a free product because the anonymized, aggregated behavioral data it collects feeds Microsoft's own machine learning research and product decisions, rather than generating direct subscription revenue. This makes Clarity an outlier among session-recording tools, most of which (Hotjar, FullStory, Crazy Egg) gate recording volume, data retention, or advanced filtering behind paid plans.

features.title: Heatmaps
features.description: Aggregate click, scroll, and area heatmaps show where visitors focus attention across a page.

features.title: Session recordings
features.description: Anonymized replays of individual visitor sessions let teams watch exactly how a user navigated a page.

features.title: Rage click and dead click detection
features.description: Automatic flags for repeated rapid clicking or clicks on non-interactive elements highlight likely friction points.

features.title: Clarity Copilot
features.description: An AI assistant that answers natural-language questions about site behavior directly from the dashboard.

features.title: Custom tags and segments
features.description: Filter recordings and heatmaps by URL, device, browser, or custom JavaScript-triggered events.

features.title: Google Analytics integration
features.description: Connects with Google Analytics and Google Analytics 360 to cross-reference recordings against existing GA segments.

features.title: Platform one-click installs
features.description: Native integrations for WordPress, Shopify, Shopify Plus, Wix, Squarespace, and WooCommerce simplify setup.

features.title: Unlimited free usage
features.description: No caps on sites, team members, or monthly tracked sessions under the single free tier.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier or feature paywall
No limits on websites, team seats, or session volume
Fast, simple setup with one-click integrations for major platforms
AI-powered Clarity Copilot speeds up finding friction points
Automatic frustration signals (rage clicks, dead clicks) surface UX issues without manual review
cons: Fewer advanced segmentation and reporting options than paid competitors like FullStory
No dedicated customer support channel beyond community and documentation
Interface and filtering are simpler than more mature paid session-recording tools
Data retention windows are shorter than some enterprise-grade paid platforms
Aggregated anonymized data is used by Microsoft to improve its own models, which some privacy-conscious teams may want to review

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Clarity really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clarity has a single free tier with no Pro, Business, or Enterprise plan, and no limits on sites, team members, or monthly sessions.

faqs.question: When was Microsoft Clarity launched?
faqs.answer: Clarity launched publicly on October 28, 2020, after starting as an internal Microsoft project around 2018.

faqs.question: Does Microsoft Clarity work with Google Analytics?
faqs.answer: Yes, Clarity integrates with Google Analytics and Google Analytics 360, allowing teams to cross-reference session recordings with existing GA segments.

faqs.question: What is Clarity Copilot?
faqs.answer: Clarity Copilot is a built-in AI assistant that lets users ask natural-language questions about site behavior, such as the top friction point on a page, and returns a generated answer.

faqs.question: Which platforms does Microsoft Clarity support out of the box?
faqs.answer: Clarity offers one-click installation for WordPress, Shopify, Shopify Plus, Wix, Squarespace, and WooCommerce, plus manual script or Google Tag Manager installation for any other site.

faqs.question: How does Microsoft make money from a free product like Clarity?
faqs.answer: Microsoft uses the anonymized, aggregated behavioral data Clarity collects to improve its own machine learning models and understand digital usage trends, rather than charging for the product directly.

use_cases.title: UX and conversion optimization
use_cases.description: Product and marketing teams use heatmaps and session recordings to identify where visitors drop off or get confused on key pages like checkout or signup flows.
use_cases.audience: UX researchers and conversion rate optimization teams

use_cases.title: E-commerce store troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Shopify and WooCommerce store owners install Clarity to watch real shopping sessions and diagnose why visitors abandon carts.

use_cases.title: SEO and content performance review
use_cases.description: SEO practitioners pair Clarity with Google Search Console data to see how visitors from organic search actually engage with landing pages.

features: Rage click and dead click detection

enrichment.short_description: Microsoft's general-purpose consumer AI chat assistant at copilot.microsoft.com, offering conversational answers, image generation, and voice interaction.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Copilot Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Copilot review covering free chat, Copilot Pro and Premium tiers, image generation, voice mode, and how it compares to ChatGPT and Gemini.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's consumer-facing conversational AI assistant, accessible at copilot.microsoft.com and built into Windows, Bing, and the Microsoft Start and Edge apps. It traces its lineage to Bing Chat, which launched on February 7, 2023 as part of a major overhaul of the Bing search engine that added a GPT-4-based chatbot to search results. Microsoft rebranded Bing Chat, Windows Copilot, and Microsoft 365 Copilot under the unified Copilot name starting in September 2023, with Bing Chat itself formally renamed Copilot at Microsoft Ignite on November 15, 2023. Microsoft Copilot is distinct from two other Microsoft products that share the Copilot name: Microsoft 365 Copilot, which is embedded inside Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams for enterprise productivity and is priced separately as a per-user add-on, and GitHub Copilot, which is a code-completion assistant for developers. The consumer Copilot at copilot.microsoft.com functions as a general-purpose chat assistant: it answers questions, drafts and summarizes text, generates images from text prompts (powered by OpenAI's image models), browses the live web for current information, and offers a voice conversation mode. It is built on OpenAI's GPT model family, with Microsoft periodically adding new capabilities such as Copilot Vision (which can view and discuss on-screen content or camera input) and, in 2026, the option to select Anthropic's Claude as an alternate model within Copilot Chat for tasks like document analysis and structured content generation. The free tier is available to anyone with a Microsoft account and includes standard chat, web-grounded answers, and limited image generation. A paid Copilot Pro subscription at $20 per month offered faster response times, priority access to the latest models, and deeper integration into Microsoft 365 apps for individual subscribers, but Microsoft retired standalone Copilot Pro in late 2025, migrating that functionality into the Microsoft 365 Personal, Family, and Premium subscription tiers instead (existing Copilot Pro subscribers can continue using it until August 1, 2026). Consumer access to advanced Copilot features going forward runs through Microsoft 365 Premium (around $19.99 per month), which folds in the AI capabilities that used to require the standalone Copilot Pro plan. Copilot's target audience spans everyday consumers using it as a free ChatGPT-style assistant, Windows and Edge users who access it natively in the OS and browser, and Microsoft 365 subscribers who want AI assistance woven into their existing apps. Its main competitors are OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude.ai, and its key differentiation is deep, no-extra-app integration into Windows, Bing, and Edge, plus free access without requiring a separate account creation for users already signed into a Microsoft account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Copilot is the company's consumer AI chat assistant, reachable directly at copilot.microsoft.com and built into Windows, Bing, and Microsoft Edge. It grew out of Bing Chat, the GPT-4-powered chatbot Microsoft added to Bing search on February 7, 2023, and was unified under the Copilot brand in late 2023.

Copilot is separate from Microsoft 365 Copilot (the enterprise productivity assistant embedded in Word, Excel, and Teams) and GitHub Copilot (a developer coding assistant), even though all three share the Copilot name and underlying AI technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core experience is a conversational chat interface that can answer questions, draft and summarize text, and browse the live web to ground answers in current information. Copilot also generates images from text prompts, offers a voice mode for spoken conversation, and includes Copilot Vision, which can analyze on-screen content or a camera feed to answer questions about what it sees.

In 2026, Microsoft added the ability to select alternate underlying models inside Copilot Chat, including Anthropic's Claude, for tasks like document understanding and structured content generation, alongside its default OpenAI-based models. A newer agentic mode, sometimes referred to as Cowork, can carry out multi-step tasks across a user's data rather than answering a single prompt at a time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Microsoft Copilot experience at copilot.microsoft.com is free to anyone with a Microsoft account, including standard chat, web-grounded answers, and limited image generation. Microsoft previously sold a standalone Copilot Pro subscription at $20 per month for individuals wanting faster responses and deeper app integration, but retired it in late 2025 in favor of folding those capabilities into Microsoft 365 subscription tiers.

Consumers who want the advanced features that used to require Copilot Pro can now get them through Microsoft 365 Personal, Family, or Premium plans, with Premium (around $19.99 per month) offering the fullest AI feature set. Separately, Microsoft 365 Copilot for business and enterprise customers is priced at roughly $30 per user per month as an add-on to a qualifying Microsoft 365 plan, and is a distinct product from the consumer Copilot covered here.

features.title: Conversational chat
features.description: Ask questions, draft text, brainstorm, and get summarized answers in natural language.

features.title: Web-grounded answers
features.description: Copilot can search the live web to ground responses in current information rather than relying only on training data.

features.title: Image generation
features.description: Generates images from text prompts using OpenAI image models.

features.title: Voice mode
features.description: Supports spoken, conversational interaction instead of typed prompts.

features.title: Copilot Vision
features.description: Can analyze on-screen content or a camera feed to answer questions about what it sees.

features.title: Selectable models
features.description: Lets users choose between Microsoft's default model and alternatives, including Anthropic's Claude, for specific tasks.

features.title: Windows and Edge integration
features.description: Built natively into Windows, Bing, and Microsoft Edge for access without a separate app.

features.title: Agentic task mode
features.description: A multi-step agentic mode can carry out longer tasks across a user's data rather than answering one prompt at a time.

pros: Free tier available to anyone with a Microsoft account
Deep native integration into Windows, Bing, and Edge without installing a separate app
Combines chat, image generation, voice, and web search in one assistant
Access to multiple underlying AI models, including Claude, in one interface
Frequent feature updates tied to Microsoft's broader AI roadmap
cons: Naming overlaps with Microsoft 365 Copilot and GitHub Copilot cause frequent user confusion
Standalone Copilot Pro was discontinued, pushing consumers toward bundled Microsoft 365 subscriptions for advanced features
Response quality and available models can vary by region and account type
Some advanced features require a Microsoft 365 subscription rather than being free
Enterprise-grade data controls require the separate Microsoft 365 Copilot product, not the consumer version

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Copilot free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Copilot experience at copilot.microsoft.com is free with a Microsoft account, including chat, web-grounded answers, and limited image generation.

faqs.question: How is Microsoft Copilot different from Microsoft 365 Copilot?
faqs.answer: Microsoft Copilot is a general-purpose consumer chat assistant, while Microsoft 365 Copilot is a separate, separately priced product embedded inside Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams for enterprise productivity tasks.

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Copilot the same as GitHub Copilot?
faqs.answer: No. GitHub Copilot is a code-completion assistant for developers inside code editors, while Microsoft Copilot is a general chat assistant for everyday tasks and questions.

faqs.question: What happened to Copilot Pro?
faqs.answer: Microsoft retired the standalone $20 per month Copilot Pro subscription in late 2025; existing subscribers can keep using it until August 1, 2026, after which advanced features move into Microsoft 365 Personal, Family, and Premium plans.

faqs.question: What AI models power Microsoft Copilot?
faqs.answer: Copilot is built primarily on OpenAI's GPT model family, and in 2026 Microsoft added the option to select alternate models, including Anthropic's Claude, within Copilot Chat.

faqs.question: What was Microsoft Copilot called before it was Copilot?
faqs.answer: It started as Bing Chat, launched February 7, 2023 as part of the redesigned Bing search engine, and was renamed Copilot at Microsoft Ignite in November 2023.

use_cases.title: Everyday question answering and research
use_cases.description: Consumers use the free chat interface for quick answers, summaries, and web-grounded research without needing a separate AI account.

use_cases.title: In-OS assistance for Windows users
use_cases.description: Windows users access Copilot directly from the taskbar for tasks like drafting text, adjusting settings, or answering questions without opening a browser.

use_cases.title: Visual and voice-driven tasks
use_cases.description: Users leverage Copilot Vision and voice mode to get help with on-screen content or hands-free conversations, such as troubleshooting an app or discussing a document aloud.

features: Copilot features integrated into personal Microsoft 365 apps
Full consumer AI feature set (successor to Copilot Pro)

enrichment.short_description: An AI-powered graphic design app from Microsoft that turns text prompts into social posts, presentations, and marketing graphics, positioned as a Canva competitor.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Designer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Designer review covering AI image generation, templates, credits, pricing, and how this Canva competitor fits into Microsoft 365.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Designer is an AI-powered graphic design application from Microsoft, publicly previewed on October 12, 2022 alongside the announcement of DALL-E 2 integration into Microsoft's consumer products, and moved to general availability in July 2024. It is Microsoft's direct answer to Canva, letting users generate complete design layouts, including images, text placement, color schemes, and decorative elements, from a plain-language description rather than starting from a blank canvas. Designer's image generation was originally powered by OpenAI's DALL-E 2 and has since been upgraded to DALL-E 3, licensed to Microsoft through its partnership with OpenAI. Beyond generating a design from scratch, Designer offers AI editing tools including background removal, object erase, auto-crop, and Restyle Image, which reimagines an uploaded photo in a new artistic style, plus AI-generated caption and copy suggestions to pair with visuals. It runs as a web app and mobile app, and its generation and editing capabilities are also woven directly into other Microsoft products, including Microsoft Photos, Word, and PowerPoint, so users can create or edit imagery without leaving the app they are already working in. Designer's pricing follows a freemium credit model: the free tier is open to anyone with a Microsoft account and includes a monthly allowance of AI generation credits (around 15 per month), which is sufficient for occasional use but limits heavier design workflows. A Microsoft 365 subscription raises that credit allowance substantially, up to roughly 4x the free amount, and unlocks broader Copilot integration across Microsoft 365 apps; the relevant subscription tiers are Microsoft 365 Personal (around $9.99/month), Family (around $12.99/month), and Premium (around $19.99/month), the last of which also absorbed the features that used to belong to the standalone Copilot Pro subscription. Designer's target audience is primarily small businesses, marketers, students, and social media managers who need quick, on-brand visual content, such as Instagram posts, event invitations, and marketing flyers, without design training or a Canva or Adobe Express subscription. Its main competitors are Canva, Adobe Express, and Adobe Firefly, and its key differentiation is native, no-extra-cost-required integration into the Microsoft 365 ecosystem (PowerPoint, Word, Photos), free access to any Microsoft account holder, and DALL-E 3 image generation baked directly into the design workflow rather than as a bolt-on feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Designer is an AI graphic design tool that generates full layouts, images, and copy suggestions from a text description. It launched in public preview on October 12, 2022, alongside Microsoft's push to integrate OpenAI's DALL-E 2 into its consumer products, and reached general availability in July 2024.

Positioned as a Canva alternative, Designer is built to work inside the Microsoft ecosystem: it is a standalone web and mobile app, but its generation and editing tools also appear inside Microsoft Photos, Word, and PowerPoint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Designer's core workflow starts with a text prompt: users describe a design concept and the app generates layouts with images, text placement, color palettes, and decorative elements already assembled. Image generation runs on DALL-E 3, an upgrade from the original DALL-E 2 integration at launch.

Editing tools include background removal and replacement, object erase, auto-crop, and Restyle Image, which applies a new artistic style to an existing photo. Designer also suggests AI-generated captions and copy to accompany visuals, and it supports templates for common formats like social posts, presentation slides, flyers, and invitations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Microsoft Designer's core app is free to any Microsoft account holder and includes a monthly allowance of around 15 AI generation credits. This is enough for light, occasional design work but limits users who need to generate many images in a month.

A Microsoft 365 subscription increases the monthly credit allowance to roughly 4x the free amount and adds broader Copilot integration across Microsoft 365 apps. The relevant tiers are Microsoft 365 Personal (around $9.99/month), Family (around $12.99/month), and Premium (around $19.99/month), with Premium offering the largest credit pool and most complete AI feature set.

features.description: Describe a design concept and Designer generates a complete layout with images, text, and color scheme.

features.title: AI caption and copy suggestions

features.description: Generation and editing tools are embedded directly inside Microsoft Photos, Word, and PowerPoint.

features.description: Pre-built templates for social posts, presentation slides, flyers, and invitations.

pros: Free tier accessible to any Microsoft account holder
DALL-E 3 image generation built directly into the design workflow
Integrates natively into PowerPoint, Word, and Microsoft Photos
AI editing tools (Restyle, background removal, object erase) go beyond basic template editing
Simple, prompt-based workflow lowers the skill barrier for non-designers
cons: Free tier credit allowance (around 15/month) is limiting for frequent use
Full credit benefits require a paid Microsoft 365 subscription
Design customization is less deep than long-established tools like Canva or Adobe Express
Smaller third-party template and asset marketplace compared to Canva
AI-generated layouts sometimes need manual cleanup for brand-specific requirements

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Designer free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core app is free with any Microsoft account and includes a monthly allowance of around 15 AI generation credits; Microsoft 365 subscriptions raise that allowance further.

faqs.question: What AI model does Microsoft Designer use for images?
faqs.answer: Designer currently uses OpenAI's DALL-E 3, an upgrade from the DALL-E 2 model it launched with in 2022.

faqs.question: When did Microsoft Designer launch?
faqs.answer: It launched in public preview on October 12, 2022, and reached general availability in July 2024.

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Designer a Canva alternative?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is widely positioned as Microsoft's answer to Canva, focused on AI-generated layouts rather than manual drag-and-drop design.

faqs.question: Does Microsoft Designer work inside PowerPoint and Word?
faqs.answer: Yes, Designer's image generation and editing tools are integrated directly into Microsoft Photos, Word, and PowerPoint, in addition to the standalone web and mobile app.

faqs.question: Do I need a Microsoft 365 subscription to use Designer?
faqs.answer: No, the app is usable for free with a Microsoft account, but a Microsoft 365 Personal, Family, or Premium subscription increases the monthly AI credit allowance.

enrichment.short_description: Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management platform, formerly Azure Active Directory, providing single sign-on, multi-factor authentication, and conditional access.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Entra ID Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Entra ID review covering Free, P1, P2 and Suite pricing, single sign-on, conditional access, and how it compares to Okta and other IAM tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud-based identity and access management (IAM) platform, previously known as Azure Active Directory (Azure AD). Microsoft announced the rename on July 11, 2023, with the name change rolling out across Microsoft experiences starting August 15, 2023, and most naming updates completing by the end of 2023; Microsoft was explicit that licensing, pricing, and functionality did not change as part of the rebrand, only the name. The underlying service traces back to Windows Server Active Directory concepts from the early 2000s, evolved into the cloud-native Azure Active Directory as part of Microsoft's Azure platform, and is now positioned as the identity layer of the broader Microsoft Entra product family, which also includes tools for permissions management and identity verification. Entra ID works by acting as a centralized cloud directory that authenticates users and enforces access policies across Microsoft 365, Azure, and thousands of third-party SaaS applications through single sign-on (SSO), rather than requiring separate logins per app. Core capabilities include single sign-on, multi-factor authentication (MFA), self-service password reset, and B2B collaboration for inviting external partners into a tenant. Higher tiers add Conditional Access (policies that grant or restrict access based on signals like device, location, and risk level), Identity Protection (machine-learning-based risk detection on sign-ins and accounts that feeds into Conditional Access), Privileged Identity Management or PIM (just-in-time elevation of administrative access instead of standing admin rights), and access reviews for periodic recertification of who has access to what. Pricing is tiered: Entra ID Free is included with any Microsoft online business service and supports up to 500,000 directory objects, SSO for up to 10 cloud apps per user, and basic reports; Entra ID P1 costs roughly $6 to $7 per user per month and adds Conditional Access and hybrid self-service password reset; Entra ID P2 costs roughly $9 to $10 per user per month and adds Identity Protection and PIM; and Microsoft Entra Suite bundles P2 with additional security add-ons for around $12 per user per month. These capabilities are also bundled into Microsoft 365 E3 (which includes P1) and Microsoft 365 E5 (which includes P2), so many enterprise customers already have some tier of Entra ID through their existing Microsoft 365 licensing. Entra ID's target audience is IT and security teams at organizations of any size that need centralized authentication and access control across cloud and on-premises applications, with heavier P1/P2 features aimed at mid-size and enterprise organizations with compliance or zero-trust security requirements. Its main competitors are Okta, Ping Identity, and Google Cloud Identity, and its key differentiation is deep native integration with Microsoft 365, Azure, and Windows, plus inclusion inside existing Microsoft 365 E3/E5 licensing that many enterprises already own.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud identity and access management platform, formerly known as Azure Active Directory. Microsoft announced the rename on July 11, 2023, rolling it out across products through the rest of that year, while stating explicitly that pricing and functionality were unchanged, only the name.

Entra ID acts as the centralized identity layer for Microsoft 365, Azure, and thousands of third-party SaaS applications, authenticating users once and enforcing access policies everywhere through single sign-on rather than requiring separate credentials per app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities available even on the free tier include single sign-on for up to 10 cloud apps per user, multi-factor authentication, self-service password change, and B2B collaboration for inviting external partners into a tenant. Paid tiers unlock Conditional Access, which grants or restricts access based on signals like device compliance, location, and calculated risk.

Entra ID P2 adds Identity Protection, which uses machine learning to flag risky sign-ins and accounts and feeds those risk scores into Conditional Access policies, plus Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time elevation of administrative access instead of standing admin rights, and access reviews for periodic recertification of who has access to sensitive resources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Entra ID Free is included with any Microsoft online business service and covers up to 500,000 directory objects, SSO for up to 10 cloud apps per user, and basic sign-in reports. Entra ID P1 (roughly $6 to $7/user/month) adds Conditional Access and hybrid self-service password reset, while Entra ID P2 (roughly $9 to $10/user/month) adds Identity Protection and Privileged Identity Management.

Microsoft Entra Suite bundles P2 with additional security add-ons for around $12/user/month. Many enterprises already have P1 or P2 included through Microsoft 365 E3 or E5 licensing respectively, since Entra ID P1 is bundled into E3 and P2 is bundled into E5.

features.title: Single sign-on (SSO)
features.description: Centralized authentication across Microsoft 365, Azure, and thousands of third-party SaaS apps.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication
features.description: Adds a second verification step, including Microsoft Authenticator and FIDO2 security keys, to sign-in.

features.title: Conditional Access
features.description: Policies that grant or restrict access based on device, location, and calculated sign-in risk.

features.title: Identity Protection
features.description: Machine-learning-based risk detection on sign-ins and user accounts, feeding risk scores into access policies.

features.title: Privileged Identity Management (PIM)
features.description: Just-in-time elevation of administrative access instead of granting standing admin rights.

features.title: Self-service password reset
features.description: Lets users reset their own passwords without IT involvement, including in hybrid on-premises/cloud setups.

features.title: B2B collaboration
features.description: Invites external partners into a tenant for secure collaboration, supported at up to 50,000 monthly active users on the free tier.

features.title: Access reviews
features.description: Periodic recertification workflows to confirm users still need the access they hold.

pros: Free tier covers SSO, MFA, and B2B collaboration for smaller organizations
Deep native integration with Microsoft 365, Azure, and Windows
Often already included via existing Microsoft 365 E3 or E5 licensing
Identity Protection and PIM provide strong zero-trust security controls at the P2 tier
Supports thousands of pre-built third-party SaaS app integrations
cons: Advanced security features (Conditional Access, PIM, Identity Protection) require paid P1 or P2 licensing
Licensing tiers and bundling with Microsoft 365 plans can be confusing to price out
Prices increased in 2026, raising the cost of P1 and P2 tiers
Feature overlap and naming changes from the Azure AD rebrand can confuse long-time admins
Full capability set is deepest for organizations already standardized on Microsoft 365 and Azure

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Entra ID the same as Azure Active Directory?
faqs.answer: Yes, Microsoft renamed Azure Active Directory to Microsoft Entra ID starting July 11, 2023; Microsoft stated that pricing and functionality were unchanged, only the name.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Microsoft Entra ID?
faqs.answer: Yes, Entra ID Free is included with any Microsoft online business service and supports up to 500,000 directory objects and SSO for up to 10 cloud apps per user.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Entra ID P1 and P2?
faqs.answer: P1 adds Conditional Access and hybrid self-service password reset; P2 adds everything in P1 plus Identity Protection and Privileged Identity Management (PIM).

faqs.question: How much does Microsoft Entra ID cost?
faqs.answer: As of 2026, P1 costs roughly $6 to $7 per user per month and P2 costs roughly $9 to $10 per user per month, with a Microsoft Entra Suite bundle around $12 per user per month.

faqs.question: Is Entra ID included in Microsoft 365?
faqs.answer: Microsoft 365 E3 includes Entra ID P1 and Microsoft 365 E5 includes Entra ID P2, so many enterprise customers already have a paid tier through existing licensing.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Microsoft Entra ID?
faqs.answer: The main competitors are Okta, Ping Identity, and Google Cloud Identity.

use_cases.title: Enterprise single sign-on
use_cases.description: IT teams deploy Entra ID to give employees one login across Microsoft 365, Azure, and third-party SaaS apps instead of separate credentials per app.
use_cases.audience: IT and security teams at mid-size to large organizations

use_cases.title: Zero-trust access control
use_cases.description: Security teams use Conditional Access and Identity Protection to enforce risk-based, device-aware access policies across the organization.

use_cases.title: Privileged access governance
use_cases.description: Organizations use Privileged Identity Management to grant admin rights only when needed rather than maintaining standing administrative access.

features: SSO for up to 10 cloud apps per user
Additional security add-ons across the Entra portfolio

enrichment.short_description: A survey, quiz, and form-building tool included with Microsoft 365 that lets users collect responses and analyze results with built-in Excel and Teams integration.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Forms Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Forms review covering free vs Microsoft 365 response limits, quiz grading, branching logic, and how it compares to Google Forms and Typeform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Forms is Microsoft's survey, quiz, and form-building tool, first released on June 20, 2016 for Office 365 Education alongside the announcement of Microsoft Classroom, before a broader public preview for commercial Office 365 users followed in 2017. It lets users build forms with up to eight question types, apply conditional branching logic that shows or hides sections based on prior answers, and collect responses that flow automatically into Excel for analysis. Forms achieved GDPR compliance in May 2017, supporting deployment in European markets, and gained integration with Microsoft Flow (later renamed Power Automate) that same year, enabling automated workflows triggered by form submissions, such as posting a Teams message or updating a SharePoint list when a response comes in. Quizzes built in Forms can be automatically graded, assigned due dates, and pushed directly into Teams assignments, which makes Forms a common substitute for dedicated quiz tools in Microsoft-standardized schools. Forms is accessible to anyone with a Microsoft account at forms.office.com. The free personal plan caps usage at 200 responses per form, 400 surveys, and 200 questions per form, which is workable for small-scale personal use but limiting for anything beyond casual polling. Upgrading to a paid Microsoft 365 Personal subscription (around $6-$10/month depending on region and billing) raises the response cap to roughly 1,000 per form, while Microsoft 365 Apps for Business and Microsoft 365 Education plans, priced as part of broader Microsoft 365 business or education licensing, support up to 5,000,000 responses, effectively removing the cap for most organizational use cases. Forms integrates natively with Excel (for response analysis), Teams (for quiz assignments and form sharing inside channels), SharePoint (for embedding forms in intranet sites), and Power Automate (for triggering downstream workflows from new responses), which is its primary differentiator versus standalone survey tools. Its limitations compared to dedicated form builders include a capped set of eight question types, comparatively basic branching logic, no custom branding on the free and lower tiers, no advanced analytics or open-text sentiment analysis, no offline data collection, and no dedicated customer support channel. Forms' target audience is Microsoft 365 users, particularly in education and corporate settings, who need quick surveys, feedback forms, event registrations, or quizzes without paying for a separate tool, especially where results need to flow directly into Excel, Teams, or a Power Automate workflow. Its main competitors are Google Forms, Typeform, SurveyMonkey, and Jotform, and its key differentiation is that it is already included at no extra cost for any organization with a Microsoft 365 or Office 365 subscription, rather than requiring a separate purchase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Forms is a survey, quiz, and form-building tool included with Microsoft 365 and Office 365. It launched on June 20, 2016 for Office 365 Education, with a broader commercial preview following in 2017, and has since become a standard tool for lightweight data collection inside organizations already using Microsoft's productivity suite.

Forms is built to be simple: users create a form with up to eight question types, share a link, and watch responses populate automatically. Its main value is not competing on advanced survey logic but on being free and pre-integrated for anyone already inside the Microsoft 365 ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Forms supports conditional branching, showing or hiding sections based on how a respondent answered a prior question, which covers most common survey design needs without requiring a more complex tool. Quizzes can be automatically graded, assigned due dates, and pushed directly into Teams assignments, a feature heavily used in education settings.

Native integrations set Forms apart from many free competitors: responses can flow straight into Excel for analysis, forms can be shared or assigned inside Teams channels, forms can be embedded in SharePoint sites, and new responses can trigger automated workflows through Power Automate, such as sending a Teams notification or updating a spreadsheet.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free personal plan is capped at 200 responses per form, 400 surveys, and 200 questions per form, which suits casual or small-scale use. A Microsoft 365 Personal subscription raises the response limit to roughly 1,000 per form.

Organizations on Microsoft 365 Apps for Business or Microsoft 365 Education plans get support for up to 5,000,000 responses per form, which removes practical limits for most business, school, and event-registration use cases, since Forms itself has no separate standalone price and is bundled into existing Microsoft 365 licensing.

features.title: Survey and form builder
features.description: Create forms with up to eight question types including choice, text, rating, and ranking questions.

features.title: Conditional branching
features.description: Show or hide sections of a form based on how a respondent answered a previous question.

features.title: Automatic quiz grading
features.description: Quizzes can be graded automatically, assigned due dates, and pushed directly into Teams assignments.

features.title: Excel response export
features.description: Responses flow directly into Excel for analysis, charting, and further processing.

features.title: Teams integration
features.description: Forms and quizzes can be shared, assigned, and completed directly inside Microsoft Teams channels.

features.title: Power Automate triggers
features.description: New form responses can trigger automated workflows, such as notifications or record updates, through Power Automate.

features.title: SharePoint embedding
features.description: Forms can be embedded directly into SharePoint intranet sites.

features.title: GDPR-compliant data handling
features.description: Forms achieved GDPR compliance in 2017, supporting deployment for European organizations and users.

pros: Included at no extra cost with any Microsoft 365 or Office 365 subscription
Deep native integration with Excel, Teams, SharePoint, and Power Automate
Automatic quiz grading makes it a strong fit for schools already on Microsoft 365
Simple, low-friction interface for building quick surveys and forms
GDPR-compliant, supporting deployment across European organizations
cons: Free personal plan is capped at 200 responses per form
Limited to eight question types compared to more flexible dedicated survey tools
No custom branding on lower-tier plans
No advanced analytics, open-text sentiment analysis, or offline data collection
No dedicated customer support channel

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Forms free?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free personal plan exists with a 200-response-per-form cap; higher response limits require a Microsoft 365 Personal, Business, or Education subscription.

faqs.question: When did Microsoft Forms launch?
faqs.answer: Forms was first released on June 20, 2016 for Office 365 Education, with a broader commercial public preview following in 2017.

faqs.question: Can Microsoft Forms grade quizzes automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, quizzes can be automatically graded, assigned due dates, and pushed directly into Microsoft Teams assignments.

faqs.question: Does Microsoft Forms integrate with Power Automate?
faqs.answer: Yes, new form responses can trigger Power Automate workflows, a capability added in 2017.

faqs.question: How many responses can Microsoft Forms handle?
faqs.answer: The free personal plan caps at 200 responses per form, Microsoft 365 Personal raises it to about 1,000, and Microsoft 365 Apps for Business or Education plans support up to 5,000,000 responses.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Microsoft Forms?
faqs.answer: The main competitors are Google Forms, Typeform, SurveyMonkey, and Jotform.

use_cases.title: Classroom quizzes and assessments
use_cases.description: Teachers create auto-graded quizzes that push directly into Teams assignments, replacing the need for a separate quiz tool.
use_cases.audience: Educators and schools on Microsoft 365 Education

use_cases.title: Employee and event feedback surveys
use_cases.description: Organizations collect feedback or event registrations with responses flowing directly into Excel for reporting.
use_cases.audience: HR teams and internal event organizers

use_cases.description: Teams use form submissions to kick off Power Automate workflows, such as approval routing or SharePoint list updates.

enrichment.short_description: A low-code application development platform from Microsoft that lets users build custom business apps with minimal coding, connected to Dataverse and hundreds of data sources.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Power Apps Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Power Apps review covering low-code app building, Dataverse, Premium and pay-as-you-go pricing, and how it compares to other low-code platforms.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Power Apps is a low-code application development platform that lets business users and professional developers build custom apps with minimal hand-written code. It became generally available in November 2016 as part of Microsoft's newly formed Power Platform, alongside what was then Microsoft Flow (now Power Automate). Power Apps works by providing a visual, drag-and-drop app designer (canvas apps) alongside a data-first, model-driven app builder (model-driven apps) that generates a full app automatically from an underlying data model in Microsoft Dataverse, Microsoft's proprietary cloud data platform. Apps built in Power Apps can pull from hundreds of standard and premium data connectors, including SharePoint, SQL Server, Excel, Salesforce, and SAP, and can be extended with custom connectors and AI Builder, a feature that adds pre-built AI models (like form processing or object detection) into an app without requiring data science expertise. Power Apps also supports Power Pages, a related capability for building external-facing, low-code websites tied to the same Dataverse backend. Pricing has shifted meaningfully in recent years: Microsoft phased out the older Per App plan (which had been priced around $5 per user per app per month) from its licensing guide in January 2026, leaving two primary paths for most customers. Power Apps Premium is priced at $20 per user per month (billed annually) and gives each licensed user unlimited access to build and run any number of apps and Power Pages sites, including premium connectors, Dataverse, and 500 AI Builder credits per user per month; large-scale enterprise deployments (2,000+ seats) can access reduced volume pricing around $12 per user per month. A Pay-As-You-Go plan charges roughly $10 per active user per app per month, billing only for users who actually open an app in a given month, which suits pilots, seasonal workers, or external vendor access where a full per-user license would be wasteful. A free Power Apps Developer Plan lets individuals build and test apps for learning and prototyping purposes, though it is not intended for production use. Any app built in Power Apps also requires the underlying user to have a Microsoft 365 license, since Power Apps is designed to sit alongside, not replace, an organization's core Microsoft 365 subscription. Power Apps' target audience spans citizen developers (non-technical business users automating a departmental process), IT teams building governed line-of-business apps, and professional developers extending apps with custom code and APIs. Its main competitors are OutSystems, Mendix, Appian, and Salesforce's low-code tools, and its key differentiation is deep integration with the rest of the Power Platform (Power Automate, Power BI, Copilot Studio) and Microsoft 365, plus a shared Dataverse backend that lets apps, automations, and reports all draw from the same governed data model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Power Apps is a low-code platform for building custom business applications, generally available since November 2016 as one of the founding products of Microsoft's Power Platform. It lets both non-technical business users and professional developers build apps with a fraction of the hand-written code required by traditional development.

Power Apps offers two primary app-building approaches: canvas apps, a visual drag-and-drop designer for full control over layout, and model-driven apps, which generate a complete app automatically from a data model defined in Microsoft Dataverse.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Apps can connect to hundreds of standard and premium data connectors including SharePoint, SQL Server, Excel, Salesforce, and SAP, and can be extended with custom connectors for internal or third-party APIs. AI Builder adds pre-built AI capabilities, such as form processing and object detection, into an app without requiring data science skills.

Power Pages extends the platform to external-facing, low-code websites built on the same Dataverse backend used by internal apps. Managed environments and governance tooling let IT admins apply data loss prevention policies and monitor app usage across an organization at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Microsoft phased out the older Per App plan from its licensing guide in January 2026, leaving Power Apps Premium ($20/user/month, billed annually) as the main per-user path, which includes unlimited apps, Dataverse, premium connectors, and 500 AI Builder credits per user per month. Large enterprise deployments of 2,000+ seats can access reduced volume pricing around $12/user/month.

A Pay-As-You-Go plan (roughly $10 per active user per app per month) charges only for users who actually open an app in a given month, which fits pilots or external/seasonal access. A free Developer Plan supports individual learning and prototyping but is not licensed for production use, and any production user still needs an underlying Microsoft 365 license.

features.title: Hundreds of data connectors

features.title: AI Builder
features.description: Adds pre-built AI models, such as form processing and object detection, into an app without data science expertise.

features.title: Power Pages
features.description: Builds external-facing, low-code websites on the same Dataverse backend used by internal apps.

features.title: Custom connectors
features.description: Extends Power Apps to connect with internal or third-party APIs not covered by standard connectors.

features.title: Managed environments and governance

pros: Deep integration with Microsoft 365, Power Automate, Power BI, and Dataverse
Supports both citizen developers and professional developers on the same platform
Hundreds of pre-built connectors reduce custom integration work
AI Builder adds AI capabilities without requiring data science skills
Pay-As-You-Go option controls cost for pilots and occasional users
cons: Removal of the cheaper Per App plan in January 2026 raised the effective entry cost for many customers
Premium connectors and Dataverse usage can add unexpected licensing costs
Requires a separate underlying Microsoft 365 license for most production users
Steeper learning curve for model-driven apps and Dataverse data modeling than pure canvas apps
Governance and environment management require dedicated IT/admin effort at scale

faqs.question: How much does Microsoft Power Apps cost?
faqs.answer: Power Apps Premium costs $20 per user per month billed annually, with reduced volume pricing around $12/user/month for enterprise deployments of 2,000+ seats; a Pay-As-You-Go plan charges roughly $10 per active user per app per month.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Power Apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Power Apps Developer Plan is free for building and testing apps, but it is intended for learning and prototyping, not production use.

faqs.question: What is the difference between canvas apps and model-driven apps?
faqs.answer: Canvas apps use a visual drag-and-drop designer for full layout control, while model-driven apps are generated automatically from a data model defined in Microsoft Dataverse.

faqs.question: When did Power Apps launch?
faqs.answer: Power Apps became generally available in November 2016 as part of the newly formed Microsoft Power Platform.

faqs.question: Do I still need a Microsoft 365 license to use Power Apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, most production users need an underlying Microsoft 365 license in addition to their Power Apps license.

faqs.question: What happened to the Power Apps Per App plan?
faqs.answer: Microsoft removed the Per App plan from its licensing guide in January 2026, making Premium (per user) and Pay-As-You-Go the two primary paths for most customers.

enrichment.short_description: Microsoft's workflow automation platform for connecting apps, automating repetitive tasks, and running attended or unattended robotic process automation (RPA) bots.
enrichment.starting_price: Included with Microsoft 365 (standard connectors); $15/user/month for Premium
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Power Automate Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Power Automate review covering cloud flows, RPA bots, per-user and per-flow pricing, and how it compares to Zapier and UiPath.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Power Automate is Microsoft's workflow automation and robotic process automation (RPA) platform, part of the Power Platform alongside Power Apps and Power BI. It launched as Microsoft Flow, first announced in April 2016 with general availability in October 2016, and was rebranded to Power Automate on November 4, 2019 at Microsoft Ignite, a change that also expanded its scope from purely cloud-based workflows to include desktop RPA and virtual agents. Power Automate works through cloud flows (automated, scheduled, or manually triggered workflows connecting cloud apps and services), desktop flows (RPA automations that record and replay actions across desktop and legacy applications), and business process flows (guided, multi-step processes for enforcing a consistent process across a team). Any organization already on Microsoft 365 can build cloud flows using standard connectors, covering commonly used Microsoft apps like Outlook, SharePoint, Teams, OneDrive, Excel, Forms, and Planner, without purchasing a separate Power Automate license. Flows that use premium connectors, such as Salesforce, SAP, or custom connectors to third-party systems, require a separate Power Automate Premium license at $15 per user per month, which also includes unlimited cloud flows, desktop flows in attended mode, 5,000 AI Builder credits, and cloud database/file storage. Unattended RPA, where bots run automatically without a human triggering or supervising each run, requires the Process plan at $150 per bot per month, or a Hosted Process plan at $215 per bot per month that includes Microsoft-hosted infrastructure so the bot doesn't need a dedicated on-premises machine. An alternative Per Flow plan, priced around $500 per month for 5 flows, is available for organizations that want to license specific high-value organizational flows rather than licensing every individual user. Process Mining, an add-on for analyzing and visualizing how existing business processes actually run in practice (as opposed to how they're documented), is available only with the Premium plan for an additional $5,000 per tenant per month, billed annually. Power Automate's target audience ranges from everyday Microsoft 365 users automating personal or team-level tasks (like saving email attachments to OneDrive) to enterprise automation and RPA teams replacing manual, repetitive back-office processes with unattended bots. Its main competitors are Zapier, UiPath, Automation Anywhere, and Make (formerly Integromat), and its key differentiation is native inclusion of basic cloud automation inside every Microsoft 365 subscription, combined with a path to enterprise-grade unattended RPA within the same platform rather than requiring a separate RPA vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Power Automate is a workflow automation and RPA platform that connects apps and automates repetitive tasks. It began as Microsoft Flow, generally available from October 2016, and was rebranded to Power Automate on November 4, 2019, a change that coincided with expanding the product beyond cloud workflows into desktop RPA and virtual agents.

The platform spans three main automation types: cloud flows for connecting online apps and services, desktop flows for RPA-style automation of desktop and legacy applications, and business process flows for enforcing consistent, guided multi-step processes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Any Microsoft 365 user can build cloud flows using standard connectors for apps like Outlook, SharePoint, Teams, OneDrive, Excel, and Planner without an extra license. Flows built with premium connectors, such as Salesforce or SAP, require a Power Automate Premium license.

For robotic process automation, desktop flows can run in attended mode (triggered by and running alongside a human user) under the Premium plan, or in unattended mode via the Process or Hosted Process plans, where bots run automatically without a supervising user. Process Mining, available as a Premium add-on, analyzes how business processes actually execute in practice to identify automation opportunities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Power Automate Premium costs $15 per user per month and includes unlimited cloud flows, attended desktop flows, 5,000 AI Builder credits, and cloud storage. Unattended RPA requires the Process plan at $150 per bot per month, or Hosted Process at $215 per bot per month for Microsoft-hosted bot infrastructure.

A Per Flow plan, around $500 per month for 5 flows, licenses specific organization-wide flows rather than individual users. Process Mining is an additional add-on available only on the Premium plan, priced at $5,000 per tenant per month billed annually.

features.title: Cloud flows
features.description: Automated, scheduled, or manually triggered workflows connecting cloud apps and services.

features.title: Desktop flows (RPA)
features.description: Record-and-replay automation of actions across desktop and legacy applications.

features.title: Unattended bots
features.description: Bots that run automatically on a schedule without a supervising human user, via the Process or Hosted Process plans.

features.title: Business process flows
features.description: Guided, multi-step processes that enforce a consistent workflow across a team.

features.title: AI Builder integration
features.description: Adds pre-built AI models, such as document processing, into automated flows.

features.title: Process Mining
features.description: Analyzes how business processes actually run in practice to surface automation opportunities.

features.title: Standard and premium connectors
features.description: Hundreds of connectors for Microsoft and third-party apps, split between included standard connectors and licensed premium connectors.

features.title: Power Platform integration
features.description: Flows can be triggered by or feed into Power Apps and Power BI within the same platform.

pros: Basic cloud automation is included with any Microsoft 365 subscription at no extra cost
Covers both simple cloud workflows and enterprise-grade unattended RPA on one platform
Deep integration with Power Apps, Power BI, and Microsoft 365 apps
AI Builder adds AI capabilities to flows without separate data science tooling
Process Mining helps identify which processes are worth automating before building flows
cons: Premium connectors require a separate $15/user/month license even for occasional use
Unattended RPA bots are expensive at $150-$215 per bot per month
Process Mining add-on is costly at $5,000 per tenant per month
Licensing model (per-user, per-flow, per-bot) can be complex to plan for at scale
Desktop flow reliability can be sensitive to UI changes in the applications being automated

faqs.question: Is Microsoft Power Automate free with Microsoft 365?
faqs.answer: Basic cloud flows using standard connectors (Outlook, SharePoint, Teams, OneDrive, Excel, Planner) are included with Microsoft 365 at no extra cost; premium connectors require a separate Premium license.

faqs.question: What was Power Automate called before?
faqs.answer: It launched as Microsoft Flow in 2016 and was renamed Power Automate on November 4, 2019 at Microsoft Ignite.

faqs.question: How much does Power Automate Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Power Automate Premium costs $15 per user per month and includes unlimited cloud flows, attended desktop flows, and 5,000 AI Builder credits.

faqs.question: What is the difference between attended and unattended RPA in Power Automate?
faqs.answer: Attended automation runs alongside a human user under the Premium plan, while unattended automation runs bots automatically without supervision and requires the Process plan ($150/bot/month) or Hosted Process plan ($215/bot/month).

faqs.question: What is Process Mining in Power Automate?
faqs.answer: Process Mining analyzes how business processes actually execute in practice, helping identify automation opportunities; it's available only as a Premium add-on for $5,000 per tenant per month.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Power Automate?
faqs.answer: The main competitors are Zapier, UiPath, Automation Anywhere, and Make (formerly Integromat).

enrichment.short_description: Microsoft's business intelligence and data visualization platform for building interactive reports and dashboards from data across an organization.
enrichment.seo_title: Microsoft Power BI Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Microsoft Power BI review covering Pro, Premium Per User and Fabric pricing, dashboards, data modeling, and how it compares to Tableau and Looker.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Microsoft Power BI is a business intelligence and data visualization platform that lets organizations build interactive reports and dashboards from data spread across many sources. It began life as Power BI for Office 365, first previewed in July 2013 and unveiled more broadly in September 2013 as an add-on tightly integrated with Excel and SharePoint. The standalone product most people recognize today, including Power BI Desktop, the cloud-based Power BI Service, and Power BI Mobile apps, officially launched on July 24, 2015. Microsoft strengthened its mobile BI capabilities the same year by acquiring Canadian company Datazen in April 2015. Power BI works by connecting to hundreds of data sources, ranging from Excel files and SQL databases to cloud services like Salesforce and Google Analytics, through Power BI Desktop, a free Windows application where users model data, define relationships, and build report visuals using the DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) formula language. Finished reports are then published to the Power BI Service, a cloud platform for sharing dashboards, scheduling data refreshes, and collaborating with colleagues. As of 2026, Power BI is used by over 350,000 organizations worldwide, and Microsoft has shipped more than 1,500 feature updates to the platform since its 2015 launch. Pricing centers on a free Power BI Desktop application for building reports, paired with paid licenses required to share and collaborate on published reports in an organization. Power BI Pro costs $14 per user per month (paid annually), a price increase from $10 that took effect in April 2025, and includes app workspace collaboration, standard refresh rates, and access to the Power BI Service. Power BI Premium Per User (PPU) costs $24 per user per month (up from $20, a roughly 20 percent increase alongside the Pro price change) and adds a much larger dataset size limit of 100 GB (versus Pro's 1 GB), up to 48 daily data refreshes instead of 8, paginated reports, deployment pipelines, and advanced AI features. Beyond per-user licensing, Microsoft also sells capacity-based Premium (P-SKU) licensing for large organizations that need to share reports broadly without every viewer needing an individual Pro license; Microsoft has been gradually steering customers from traditional Premium capacity toward its newer Fabric capacity SKUs at renewal, positioning Fabric as the long-term successor for large-scale analytics workloads. Power BI's target audience spans business analysts and data teams building reports, up through IT and data platform teams managing enterprise-wide dashboards and governed datasets, and it is especially popular among organizations already invested in the Microsoft 365 and Azure ecosystem. Its main competitors are Tableau, Google Looker, and Qlik Sense, and its key differentiation is a free desktop authoring tool, tight integration with Excel and the rest of Microsoft 365, and a licensing on-ramp that scales from a single Pro seat up to enterprise-wide capacity or Fabric licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Microsoft Power BI is a business intelligence platform for building interactive reports and dashboards. It began as Power BI for Office 365, previewed in July 2013, before the standalone product most people know today, including Power BI Desktop, the Power BI Service, and mobile apps, launched on July 24, 2015.

Power BI connects to hundreds of data sources and lets users model relationships and build visuals using DAX, Microsoft's formula language for data analysis. As of 2026, it is used by over 350,000 organizations, reflecting its position as one of the most widely adopted BI platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Power BI Desktop, a free Windows application, is where most report-building happens: connecting to data sources, modeling relationships between tables, and designing report visuals. Finished reports publish to the Power BI Service, the cloud platform used to share dashboards, schedule data refreshes, and collaborate with colleagues.

Higher tiers unlock larger dataset limits, more frequent data refreshes, paginated (pixel-perfect, print-ready) reports, deployment pipelines for managing report versions across development and production, and advanced AI-powered analytics features built into the report canvas.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Power BI Desktop is free for building reports individually. Power BI Pro costs $14 per user per month (paid annually), following an April 2025 price increase from $10, and is required for sharing and collaborating on reports inside an organization.

Power BI Premium Per User costs $24 per user per month, up from $20, and raises the dataset size limit to 100 GB, increases refreshes to 48 per day, and unlocks paginated reports and advanced AI features. Larger organizations can also license capacity-based Premium or Fabric SKUs, with Microsoft gradually shifting customers toward Fabric capacity at renewal as its long-term large-scale analytics offering.

features.title: Power BI Desktop
features.description: A free Windows application for connecting to data, modeling relationships, and building report visuals.

features.title: DAX formula language
features.description: Data Analysis Expressions let users build custom calculations and measures within data models.

features.title: Power BI Service
features.description: A cloud platform for publishing, sharing, and scheduling refreshes of finished reports and dashboards.

features.title: Hundreds of data connectors
features.description: Connects to Excel, SQL databases, Salesforce, Google Analytics, and hundreds of other data sources.

features.title: Paginated reports
features.description: Pixel-perfect, print-ready report formats available on Premium tiers.

features.title: Deployment pipelines
features.description: Manage report versions across development, test, and production environments.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native mobile apps for viewing dashboards and reports on the go.

features.title: AI-powered analytics
features.description: Built-in AI features for natural-language querying and automated insights, expanded on Premium tiers.

pros: Power BI Desktop is free for individual report building
Tight integration with Excel and the broader Microsoft 365/Azure ecosystem
Used by over 350,000 organizations, indicating a mature ecosystem and community
Scales from a single Pro seat to enterprise-wide capacity or Fabric licensing
Frequent feature updates, with over 1,500 shipped since its 2015 launch
cons: Pro and Premium Per User prices increased significantly in April 2025 ($10 to $14 and $20 to $24)
Sharing and collaborating on reports requires a paid Pro or Premium license even for viewers in some configurations
Capacity-based Premium licensing is being phased toward Fabric, adding complexity for long-term planning
DAX and data modeling have a real learning curve for non-technical users
Large-scale enterprise analytics increasingly pushes customers toward the pricier Fabric platform

faqs.question: Is Power BI free?
faqs.answer: Power BI Desktop, the report-building application, is free; sharing and collaborating on published reports inside an organization requires a paid Pro or Premium Per User license.

faqs.question: How much does Power BI Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Power BI Pro costs $14 per user per month paid annually, following an April 2025 increase from the previous $10 price.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Power BI Pro and Premium Per User?
faqs.answer: Premium Per User ($24/user/month) raises the dataset size limit to 100 GB versus Pro's 1 GB, increases daily refreshes to 48 from 8, and adds paginated reports and advanced AI features.

faqs.question: When did Power BI launch?
faqs.answer: Power BI began as Power BI for Office 365 in 2013, with the standalone product most people use today launching on July 24, 2015.

faqs.question: What is Microsoft Fabric's relationship to Power BI?
faqs.answer: Microsoft Fabric is Microsoft's newer, broader analytics platform, and Power BI capacity licensing is gradually being shifted toward Fabric capacity SKUs at renewal for large-scale deployments.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Power BI?
faqs.answer: The main competitors are Tableau, Google Looker, and Qlik Sense.

use_cases.title: Business reporting and dashboards
use_cases.description: Analysts build interactive dashboards that let stakeholders explore sales, marketing, or operations data without needing a dedicated analyst for every question.

use_cases.title: Enterprise data governance and self-service BI
use_cases.description: IT and data platform teams create governed datasets that business users can safely build their own reports on top of.
use_cases.audience: Data platform and IT teams at large organizations

use_cases.title: Excel-integrated financial and operational analysis
use_cases.description: Finance and operations teams already working in Excel extend their analysis into interactive Power BI reports using the same underlying data.

features: Connect to hundreds of data sources
No sharing/publishing to the cloud service
Publish and share reports via Power BI Service
